content.text: What Is Toil in a GenAI SRE Context

Toil is manual, repetitive, tactical work that scales linearly with service load and produces no lasting improvement. Google SRE defines it operationally: if you had to do this work last sprint and you will have to do it again next sprint without the work becoming easier, it is toil. For GenAI services, toil has a distinctive shape because the work is driven by model behaviour, provider contracts, and probabilistic outputs rather than deterministic software bugs.

The classic examples for a GenAI team include manually re-running embedding pipelines after a schema change, hand-tuning token budgets when request shapes shift, acknowledging noisy safety-classifier alerts that fire on benign prompts, and preparing usage reports for finance every quarter. None of this work makes the system better — it keeps it operational while the real improvements wait.

SRE practice targets spending fewer than 50 % of engineering hours on toil. When toil exceeds that threshold, the team cannot invest in reliability improvements, and the system degrades over time. This threshold is not a guideline — it is a gate that triggers escalation to engineering leadership.

attrs.alt: Toil taxonomy and reduction framework diagram

content.text: Toil is often invisible until it is measured. Engineers absorb it as "just part of the job", and because each individual task is small it never shows up in incident reports. A structured audit breaks that invisibility.

At the end of each sprint, each engineer answers three questions: What did I do that took meaningful time, required human judgment, and would need to happen again in a future sprint? The answers are submitted to a shared tracker and tagged by category. This takes ten minutes per person and produces a normalised time estimate per category across the team.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from datetime import date
from typing import List


class ToilCategory(Enum):
    OPERATIONAL = "operational"
    PIPELINE = "pipeline"
    INCIDENT = "incident"
    COMPLIANCE = "compliance"
    ON_CALL = "on_call"


class ToilResolution(Enum):
    ELIMINATE = "eliminate"
    AUTOMATE = "automate"
    REDUCE = "reduce"
    TRACK = "track"   # accepted for now; must be reviewed quarterly


@dataclass
class ToilEntry:
    engineer: str
    sprint: str           # e.g. "2024-Q1-S3"
    category: ToilCategory
    description: str
    hours_spent: float
    recurrence: str       # "weekly" | "per-deploy" | "per-incident" | "monthly"
    automation_blocked_by: str = ""   # empty means automation is feasible

    def annualised_hours(self) -> float:
        multiplier = {"weekly": 50, "per-deploy": 26, "per-incident": 8,
                      "monthly": 12}.get(self.recurrence, 4)
        return self.hours_spent * multiplier

    def resolution_priority(self) -> ToilResolution:
        if not self.automation_blocked_by:
            return ToilResolution.AUTOMATE
        if self.hours_spent > 4:
            return ToilResolution.REDUCE
        return ToilResolution.TRACK

Incident management systems (PagerDuty, OpsGenie) expose a signal that teams routinely ignore for toil purposes: the alert-to-action log. Every alert that was acknowledged and resolved without changing any code is a potential toil candidate. Run a weekly query against the log to surface the top repeated manual responses.

import sqlite3
from collections import Counter
from typing import NamedTuple


class AlertToilSummary(NamedTuple):
    alert_name: str
    occurrences: int
    avg_resolution_minutes: float
    total_engineer_hours: float
    classification: str   # "toil" | "incident" | "requires_review"


def classify_alert_toil(db_path: str, days: int = 14) -> list[AlertToilSummary]:
    """
    Query on-call log; classify alerts where every resolution was manual
    (no code change, no ticket opened) as toil candidates.
    """
    conn = sqlite3.connect(db_path)
    rows = conn.execute("""
        SELECT alert_name,
               COUNT(*)                            AS occurrences,
               AVG(resolution_minutes)             AS avg_min,
               SUM(resolution_minutes) / 60.0      AS total_hrs,
               MAX(code_change_deployed)           AS any_code_change,
               MAX(ticket_opened)                  AS any_ticket
        FROM on_call_events
        WHERE ts >= DATE('now', ? || ' days')
        GROUP BY alert_name
        ORDER BY total_hrs DESC
    """, (f"-{days}",)).fetchall()
    conn.close()

    results = []
    for name, occ, avg_min, total_hrs, code_chg, ticket in rows:
        if not code_chg and not ticket:
            classification = "toil"
        elif occ > 5:
            classification = "requires_review"
        else:
            classification = "incident"
        results.append(AlertToilSummary(name, occ, avg_min, total_hrs, classification))
    return results

Runbooks that are executed more than once per sprint for the same alert are almost always toil. Tag each runbook execution in your wiki (Confluence, Notion) and run a monthly report. A runbook executed more than eight times per month with no alert-suppression outcome is a reduction target.
Generic SRE toil taxonomy applies, but GenAI workloads introduce categories that have no counterpart in traditional services.
Model version bump or traffic shape change
Config driven; auto-tune from p99 latency
Automated pipeline trigger on model-registry event
Automated threshold search via offline eval set
Cache keyed on model hash; auto-invalidation
Eval harness with confidence scoring gates

Without measurement, toil is invisible and reduction programs have no baseline to improve. The toil budget is expressed as a fraction of engineering capacity spent on toil in a given period. Capacity is typically measured as available engineer-sprint-hours, excluding vacation, interviews, and company meetings.
Identifying toil is half the work. The reduction program turns findings into funded engineering work tracked on a dedicated backlog.

Set a quarterly objective: reduce toil budget from its current percentage to a target. Each key result is a specific automation or elimination that removes a measurable number of hours. This frames toil reduction as engineering work, not heroics.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List, Optional
from enum import Enum


class TicketStatus(Enum):
    BACKLOG = "backlog"
    IN_PROGRESS = "in_progress"
    DONE = "done"
    BLOCKED = "blocked"


@dataclass
class ToilReductionTicket:
    ticket_id: str
    toil_category: str
    description: str
    strategy: str                  # eliminate | automate | reduce
    estimated_hours_saved_per_sprint: float
    estimated_engineering_cost_hours: float
    status: TicketStatus = TicketStatus.BACKLOG
    blocking_reason: Optional[str] = None

    @property
    def roi_sprints(self) -> float:
        """Sprints until the automation pays for itself."""
        if self.estimated_hours_saved_per_sprint == 0:
            return float("inf")
        return self.estimated_engineering_cost_hours / self.estimated_hours_saved_per_sprint

    def is_quick_win(self) -> bool:
        return self.roi_sprints <= 2.0

    def ticket_line(self) -> str:
        roi = f"{self.roi_sprints:.1f} sprints ROI"
        flag = " [QUICK WIN]" if self.is_quick_win() else ""
        return (f"{self.ticket_id}: {self.description} | "
                f"saves {self.estimated_hours_saved_per_sprint:.1f}h/sprint | "
                f"{roi}{flag} | {self.status.value}")


def prioritise_reduction_backlog(
    tickets: List[ToilReductionTicket],
) -> List[ToilReductionTicket]:
    """Sort by ROI (fastest payback first); blocked tickets go last."""
    active = [t for t in tickets if t.status != TicketStatus.BLOCKED]
    blocked = [t for t in tickets if t.status == TicketStatus.BLOCKED]
    active.sort(key=lambda t: t.roi_sprints)
    return active + blocked
Several recurring GenAI toil patterns have well-established automation shapes.
Instead of manually adjusting token limits after each model bump, instrument p95/p99 completion token usage and run a feedback loop that adjusts the budget cap to stay within the target percentile.

import statistics


def compute_token_budget(
    recent_completion_tokens: list[int],
    target_percentile: float = 0.95,
    safety_margin: float = 1.15,
    hard_max: int = 4096,
) -> int:
    """
    Derive a token budget from observed completion lengths.
    Replaces the sprint ritual of manually bumping max_tokens after model changes.
    """
    if not recent_completion_tokens:
        return hard_max

    sorted_tokens = sorted(recent_completion_tokens)
    idx = int(len(sorted_tokens) * target_percentile)
    p_value = sorted_tokens[min(idx, len(sorted_tokens) - 1)]
    budget = int(p_value * safety_margin)
    return min(budget, hard_max)


# Usage: call weekly from a cron job; write result to config store.
# recent = fetch_last_7_days_completion_tokens(service="chat-api")
# new_budget = compute_token_budget(recent)
# config_store.set("max_completion_tokens", new_budget)
Full index rebuilds are expensive and create a weekly manual task. An incremental approach adds only changed documents, cutting rebuild cost by 80–95 % on typical corpora.

Safety classifier threshold tuning is high-toil because every false-positive spike triggers a manual investigation and threshold adjustment. Automate this by running a threshold grid search against a labelled offline eval set whenever the classifier model is updated.

import statistics
from dataclasses import dataclass


@dataclass
class ThresholdSearchResult:
    threshold: float
    false_positive_rate: float
    false_negative_rate: float
    f1_score: float


def find_optimal_threshold(
    scores: list[float],
    labels: list[int],           # 1 = truly unsafe, 0 = safe
    fpr_budget: float = 0.02,    # max tolerable false-positive rate
) -> ThresholdSearchResult:
    """
    Grid-search classifier threshold. Maximises F1 subject to FPR <= fpr_budget.
    Replaces the manual threshold-tuning sprint ritual.
    """
    best: ThresholdSearchResult | None = None

    for raw_t in range(1, 100):
        t = raw_t / 100.0
        tp = sum(1 for s, l in zip(scores, labels) if s >= t and l == 1)
        fp = sum(1 for s, l in zip(scores, labels) if s >= t and l == 0)
        fn = sum(1 for s, l in zip(scores, labels) if s < t and l == 1)
        tn = sum(1 for s, l in zip(scores, labels) if s < t and l == 0)

        total_neg = fp + tn or 1
        fpr = fp / total_neg
        if fpr > fpr_budget:
            continue

        precision = tp / (tp + fp) if (tp + fp) > 0 else 0.0
        recall = tp / (tp + fn) if (tp + fn) > 0 else 0.0
        f1 = (2 * precision * recall / (precision + recall)
              if (precision + recall) > 0 else 0.0)

        if best is None or f1 > best.f1_score:
            best = ThresholdSearchResult(t, fpr, fn / (tp + fn or 1), f1)

    return best

Reduction programs stall without accountability. Every quarter publish a toil reduction report that shows the starting toil percentage, the target, and the actual result. Each completed ticket cites the hours saved per sprint so the impact is visible.
Measuring toil once and never updating.
Toil patterns shift every time a model is upgraded or a new feature is launched. Sprint surveys must be recurring, not a one-off audit.
An automation that breaks silently and requires manual restart is itself toil. Any automation that runs unsupervised must have alerting, retry logic, and a rollback path.
Accepting provider toil as fixed cost.
API key rotations, usage report generation, and vendor SLA reviews feel immovable but most can be automated or at minimum templated. Treat them as first-class toil, not overhead.
Not counting on-call toil in the budget.
Alert acknowledgement, runbook execution, and manual status page updates are toil even when they happen outside sprint work. Count all engineer time, including pager hours.
Engineers default to automating toil because automation feels productive. Ask elimination first: would this toil disappear if we changed the architecture or dropped a low-value feature?

Toil identification and reduction is a structured engineering discipline, not a morale program. For GenAI services, toil is amplified by model lifecycle churn, probabilistic outputs, and compliance requirements that change faster than the code. The reduction program works by making toil visible through sprint surveys and on-call log analysis, categorising it using a consistent taxonomy, and treating each reduction ticket as engineering work with a measurable ROI. Keeping the team below 50 % toil preserves the capacity needed to build reliable systems rather than just maintain them.
